quotes.verbatim_quote:
First inversion triads with the upper voice motion sub are the exact same notation as root triads with the upper voice motion
super—but of course the tonic first step triad will appear on a different numbered scale step as the examples show:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the duplication principle that lets Van Eps later omit root-position upper-voice-super studies, a structural pruning rule for the whole series.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
As was mentioned earlier, notice how 1st inversion triads with the upper voice sub produce
Root triads on the 1st and 3rd beats of each measure
quotes.why_load_bearing: Points out the audible by-product of the upper-voice-sub motion—root triads on strong beats—that ties the abstract voice motion to a perceivable harmonic event.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The two notes in the moving upper line of each step move from the tonic to the next diatonic step of the scale
notationwise as usual, except that they are now sounded on two strings instead of the usual one, which makes a four string
overall spread. In other words, the single step melodic interval is sounded on two strings—one note per string.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the four-string articulated triad geometry that distinguishes this section's voicings—a single melodic step distributed across two strings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The timing of the articulating fingers must be very precise because the two notes in the moving line must not be heard ringing
together, they must be very closely linked but not ring over each other. When properly executed just a trio of tones is heard,
never four, unless a hold-over effect is desired.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the audible test for correct four-string-spread execution: three sounding tones, never four—the listener-side criterion for the technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The first joint of the second finger plays the double stop in flattened position (just the first joint flat, not the whole finger).
and at the precise moment must be arched into hammer position while making certain that the middle note “A” on the D
string is sustained—the note must be held full value.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the exact joint-level mechanic—flatten-then-arch the second finger's first joint—that produces the lap-piano sustain under articulated upper voices.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The action of raising the flattened first joint of the second finger to arched position
and lowering the first finger must be simultaneous and smooth as though they were mechanically linked together and
therefore unable to be either up or down at the same time.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the 'mechanical linkage' image as the mental model for coordinated articulating fingers, applicable across all four-string-spread triads.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A smoothness is gained by a mixture of both the 4 string articulation and the 3 string fingering in
various places as this example shows
quotes.why_load_bearing: Authorizes mixing the four-string and three-string spreads within a single passage, a key flexibility rule for real-world application of the articulated-triad concept.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The articulating fingers must work as a precision team
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distills the chapter's articulation discipline into a single rule—finger coordination, not finger independence, is the operative virtue.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Practice the articulation reversed as shown below
quotes.why_load_bearing: Mandates bidirectional practice of the articulated triad, completing the technique's symmetric demands beyond the written ascent.
